data.title: Vltava Fund Q2 2025 Letter To Shareholders
data.description:
SHIPS ON THE WAVES

When people ask me why I invest in stocks, I tell them that it is because I’m trying to reduce certain risks in my life. Investing in stocks is often associated in the public mind with maximizing returns or with risky speculation. Experience tells me, however, that sophisticated investors think differently. In their eyes, investments serve a much more important function. They see investments as a tool for managing and reducing financial risks, and most especially risks associated with long-term currency depreciation and inflation. Many people tend to understand investing in stocks as something that brings risk rather than something that reduces risk. Yes, investing in stocks, like any other activity, carries with it certain risks. But taking reasonable risks can in return reduce other, much more substantial long-term risks that investors face.

Vltava Fund is a fund for qualified investors. The definition as to who is regarded as a qualified investor changes over time. One of the current conditions is that every such investor must have a certain relatively large amount of assets. Our investors meet this condition, and some of them have truly vast assets. This puts most of them in a somewhat different position compared to ordinary retail investors with regard to why they invest and what they want to achieve. In addition to their desire to realize an attractive return, most of them also strive to protect the value of the assets they already have – not only for themselves but often also for future generations.

If a person has greater wealth and holds it in cash or other low-yielding assets, this may seem safe at first glance, but the long-term, systematic, and essentially inevitable devaluation of the currency will exist as a constant threat. The real value of their savings is very likely to decline significantly over time. It can be said that a person who has greater assets automatically becomes an investor, because even the decision not to invest has a major impact on the real value of his or her assets.

The impact of currency devaluation is very much evident with the passage of time. Over the past 80 years (that is to say in one human lifetime), cumulative inflation in the US has exceeded 1,500% (Source: Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis). Simply put, what 80 years ago cost $10 would cost more than $150 today. The US currency has lost more than 90% of its value during that time. The dollar is the main global reserve currency, and yet its value is rapidly declining. A handful of countries (for example, Switzerland and Japan) are doing slightly better, but most developed countries are even worse off (the UK, Australia, France), and that’s not even to mention the many countries such as Turkey, Russia, Argentina, Brazil, and Israel. The future does not look bright when one considers the rates at which most currencies are depreciating. Indeed, quite the opposite. Structural deficits, growing government debt, and long-term expansionary monetary policies suggest that currency devaluation will continue to be the main risk with which investors must contend. Investing in stocks can help them to do just that. Exactly 80 years ago, the S&P 500 stock index (SP500, SPX) stood at 14.87. As I write these lines, its value is 6,092. That is more than 400 times higher. Taking into account and reinvesting all dividends (which is largely just a theoretical exercise), the total return would be even higher, exceeding 5,000 times. Using the US market in these comparisons amounts to a bit of “data mining” inasmuch as few other markets have similarly high 80-year real returns, but the argument about stock returns compared to the rate of currency depreciation is valid also more generally.

Vltava Fund is an equity fund. Therefore, we do not consider which asset classes to invest in as part of our investment considerations. We see our fund as a product intended for the equity portion of our individual investors’ asset allocations. When selecting individual stocks for the Vltava Fund portfolio, however, we constantly remind ourselves of the long-term goals for most of our investors. We try to put aside what is happening today and instead visualize what may happen in a few years. Rather than to consider a speculative horizon of less than 90 days (3 months), we prefer to consider an investment horizon longer than 1,090 days (3 years). Market developments often tempt us to think in a manner that is too short-term in nature, and we believe that to be a mistake. The past few months have provided a case in point. The market has been hit by dramatic statements that would make it easy to succumb to emotion, to overreact, or to try and time the market.

You may have noticed that our letters to shareholders do not very often dive into politics. It is not our tendency to overestimate the extent to which politics influence the stock markets. Oftentimes, people misjudge in just such a manner. The main economic trends and, above all, significant developments in individual companies span several governments and often defy party labels. If I count correctly, in the 21 years of Vltava Fund’s existence, we have already lived through seven US presidential terms. As for some of the other countries in which we have long-term investments, the UK has had 8 prime ministers in that time, Germany 4 chancellors, Japan 11 prime ministers, and the Czech Republic 9 prime ministers.

When I look back at our own investments, the most common denominator among those that have been most successful was that these were high-quality, well-managed companies whose value climbed markedly over time. The development of their share prices then favorably reflected both the growth in the company’s value and its undervaluation at the time of purchase. In the case of investments that did not work out for us, the most common denominator was that our perception of the company’s quality and value had been mistaken. I cannot recall a single case where we decided to buy or sell an investment based upon who was or was not the president or prime minister in a given country at the time. Politics influence global developments, but in long-term investing, as we understand and practice it at Vltava Fund, we consider it much more important to focus on analyzing individual companies and looking for those that have strong competitive advantages, capable management, strong free cash flow, potential for long-term growth, and effective asset allocation. These considerations, combined with an emphasis on attractive stock valuations, allow us to look beyond the daily news and price charts and to benefit from an advantage that cannot easily be quantified but is nevertheless very valuable — the advantage of patience and a long-term perspective.

The saying “what doesn’t kill you makes you stronger” can be applied to high-quality companies. The world is constantly changing and evolving. The economic, political, regulatory, and competitive environments within which individual companies operate are in a state of continuous change. We look upon companies as living organisms in which people create value through their work and at the same time influence their capability to adapt to changing business conditions. Better companies tend to have greater adaptability and overall resilience, and very often they emerge stronger from the maelstrom of events. Lower quality companies many times face deterioration in their market positions, marginalization, and sometimes even existential challenges. We have seen examples of such development many times in the markets, which is why we at Vltava Fund strive to look beyond the horizon of current events when selecting investments. In valuing companies, we emphasize their abilities to adapt to change, to innovate, and at the same time to remain true to their corporate cultures. We believe that the key to long-term success is not to try to predict every wave that will sweep across the market but to find ships that are built to sail even through storms. If you look at our portfolio, I believe you will agree that it is dominated by companies possessing just these qualities. Such companies allow us to take advantage of something that the market often disregards: time.

Time and a longer perspective also provide for another opportunity that markets offer to long-term investors. The vast majority of both amateur and professional investors and speculators operating in the stock markets have time horizons that are too short. They try to find stocks where they feel there is a chance for quick profits. At the same time, they tend to ignore stocks where the hope of getting rich quickly is small but the probability of attractive long-term returns is high. This activity creates great competition at the short end of the market; but, as the market lengthens, competition diminishes. Taking advantage of this phenomenon is sometimes referred to as time arbitrage. If you look at the individual stocks in the Vltava Fund portfolio, almost all of them offer little hope of getting rich quick but a high probability of attractive long-term returns. It seems to me that, as the years go by, the conditions for taking advantage of time arbitrage are improving more and more, and we are striving to take maximum advantage of this. It makes a lot of sense to us. Such investments do not require taking on a lot of risk.

Changes in the portfolio

We added no new companies to our portfolio this past quarter, nor did we get rid of any. Nevertheless, we were rather active and focused on buying additional shares, especially during April, when the prices of some stocks were so low that we couldn’t even believe our eyes. Usually, that is a good sign. We strengthened our positions in 10 different companies, which represent half of our portfolio. In percentage terms, we increased our holdings most in Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), United Rentals (URI), and Arrow Electronics (ARW). We also added to our position in the Nikkei 225 index. (NKY:IND)

We welcome the volatility that this year’s events have brought to the markets. The more stock prices fluctuate, the more they deviate from their fundamental values (in both directions) and the more often they give us opportunity to take advantage of this. As a result of our long-term analytical work, we have a list of companies that we like and whose fundamental values we understand. We are continuously expanding and updating this list, inclusive of our assessments of fundamental values. This, then, is a kind of shopping list of stocks, and currently it comprises about 100 companies. Most of these stocks are trading most of the time at prices that are too high for us to buy. We are accustomed to waiting patiently for some of their prices to fall convincingly below the companies’ intrinsic values. Greater market volatility means that this favorable situation arises more frequently. Even during relatively calm times, it is normal for the price of a typical stock to fluctuate within a range of 40% of its initial price during the year. This does not require any dramatic developments within any given company. In contrast, the fundamental value of a company usually changes much more slowly. The volatility of share prices is several times greater than is that in the development of fundamental share values. Our approach to the markets is based on this fact, which has been proven many times over. It is founded on an activity known as “pricing” (meaning to take advantage of a favorable ratio between the share price and a stock’s value) and avoids an activity termed “timing” (meaning to attempt at predicting market movements and trying to profit from them in the short term). Pricing is a much easier and more logical way to invest money in stocks than is timing.

While trying to predict short-term market developments, it is not enough in and of itself for one correctly to estimate what the immediate political, economic, and other developments will look like. It is necessary also to know what other investors are expecting and to what extent this is already reflected in prices. Imagine that you have been invited to a Ball of Illusions. In a luxurious hall full of mirrors and soft whispers, a hundred guests gather to take part in a beauty contest organized by a prominent patron. Six ladies in silk dresses, each beautiful in her own way, stand on the stage. But the winner will not be chosen based upon who the individual spectators like the most. No. The task of each spectator is to choose the lady they think the others will choose as the most beautiful. Sounds strange? That’s the magic of it. This is a Keynesian beauty contest. Economist John Maynard Keynes used it as a metaphor for investor behavior in the markets. When investors buy stocks, often they do not choose the one that might be seen as having the greatest real value but rather the one they think others will think has value. And that’s a game of illusions — just like at the ball.

We avoid short-term timing and playing with illusions. Pricing is at the heart of our considerations. We are aware that estimates of a company’s intrinsic value necessarily embody certain elements of subjectivity, but, by taking a conservative approach to valuation and ensuring that there is a margin of safety between the price and the value of the share, it is possible to invest money in shares calmly and methodically without having to take great risks. In this way, the speculative element in investing is kept to a minimum. We hope that the markets will remain volatile in the future and that we will be able to take advantage of this. This year has brought a few opportunities. For example, when Williams-Sonoma’s (WSM) share price was above $200 at the beginning of the year, it was significantly higher than our estimate of its intrinsic value. We sold the shares. Conversely, when United Rentals’ share price was below $600 in March and April, it was significantly lower than our estimate of its intrinsic value. We have now added it to our portfolio. These are just two examples from this year, but they illustrate how we make decisions about individual transactions. For shares that we hold or are potentially interested in owning, we monitor the ratio between their prices and values on a daily basis.

Our approach to markets and investments may sometimes seem unusual. We find joy in periods when the market is highly uncertain. This is because stock prices are usually more attractive during such periods. Times of maximum uncertainty usually bring the best prices. History provides us with countless examples. Conversely, when investors believe that the markets are heading for smooth sailing, prices tend to be high and opportunities few and far between. Low stock prices not only benefit us in our investments, but also provide better opportunities for intelligent and effective investments to be made by the companies in our portfolio. In such an environment, it is easier to create value for companies and value in the portfolio. Higher volatility, higher uncertainty, and lower prices – that is the ideal combination I would like to see. Our shopping list is ready, and so are we.

Daniel Gladiš

Disclosure: https://www.vltavafund.com/disclaimer

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Commodities: Drone Attacks Disrupt Kurdish Oil Supply
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - Middle distillate tightness persists

Oil prices firmed yesterday despite a recovery in the USD amid waning concern that President Trump may remove Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell from his position. Some fairly supportive US macro data also supported oil. US retail sales came in stronger than expected, while initial jobless claims were lower than expected.

Near-term oil fundamentals remain supportive, with the market set to remain fairly tight through this quarter, before becoming better supplied from the last three months of the year. In addition, drone attacks on oil fields in Kurdistan provided some further support, with producers suspending operations, resulting in around 200k b/d of lost production. However, a deal between the government in Baghdad and the Kurdistan regional government should resume oil exports from Kurdistan, after being halted since early 2023. The Kurdish region will supply Iraq’s State Organization for Marketing of Oil (SOMO) with at least 230k b/d.

The middle distillates market continues to signal tightness, with the ICE gasoil crack trading above US$26/bbl now. Meanwhile, Insights Global data shows that gasoil inventories in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) region fell by 86kt week on week to 1.76mt. This is the lowest level since January 2024. The strength in the European middle distillate market is pulling in diesel from further afield, with reports of some shipments from East Asia to Europe. The strength in middle distillate cracks is providing a boost to refinery margins, which should see refiners increase run rates, helping to ease the tightness. In addition, OPEC+ supply increases will increase the availability of medium sour crude.

US natural gas prices saw little change yesterday, with EIA storage data showing that US gas storage increased by 46bcf over the last week, not too different from the 45bcf increase the market was expecting. This leaves total US natural gas storage at 3.05tcf, 6.2% above the 5-year average but down 4.9% year on year.

After initially rallying, European natural gas prices came under pressure towards the end of the day, with the Title Transfer Facility (TTF) settling 1.15% lower. Unplanned outages at the Nyhamna and Kollsnes processing plants in Norway provided support to European prices earlier in the week. Grid data from the UK shows that these flows are improving.

Agriculture - Cocoa prices slump on weaker grindings

Cocoa prices came under further pressure yesterday, with London cocoa settling more than 7% lower on the day and at its lowest level since February 2024. This is after grinding data from Europe, Asia, and North America showed large declines, signalling weaker demand. The high price environment seen in cocoa has led to demand destruction in an attempt to balance the market.

In Europe, data from the European Cocoa Association shows that cocoa grindings fell 7.2% YoY (-6.2% quarter on quarter) to 331.8kt in the second quarter of 2025, the lowest level since 2020. Similarly, Cocoa Association of Asia data shows that Asian cocoa grindings fell 16.3% YoY (-17.4%QoQ) to 176.6kt in the second quarter, the lowest level since 2017. Meanwhile, the National Confectioners Association reports that grindings in North America fell by a more modest 2.78% YoY to 101.9kt in the second quarter.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed Income Monitor: July 2025
data.description:
By Columbia Threadneedle Investments Fixed Income Investment Team

Our proprietary Fixed Income Monitor compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income. It’s designed to help investors identify opportunities and risks in the asset class.

Key takeaways for July 2025

  • Economic data is pointing to softer trends in both inflation dynamics and the labor market, boosting confidence that the Federal Reserve will resume its cutting path sooner rather than later. Two-year and 30-year Treasury yields declined 18 and 16 basis points, respectively, as investors fully priced in odds of a September rate cut.
  • Risk assets appeared unphased by moderating economic activity and renewed geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. High-yield corporate bonds led performance across credit sectors as risk premiums continued to tighten, having retraced the full effect of widening between February and early April.
  • The municipal market easily digested the largest June new issue supply on record - and fifth largest monthly supply ever - while posting positive returns and seeing spreads relatively unchanged. Attractive taxable equivalent yields are enticing more crossover buyers, aiding in absorption of supply.

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments, as of June 30, 2025. Each bar represents the range for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Yield is represented by yield to worst, which is the minimum return received on a callable bond, apart from the yield if the issuer were to default. Yield percentile and spread percentile are represented by the range of daily yields and daily spreads, respectively, and both are for the last 20 years, with the current percentile position indicated. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

Interpreting yields and spreads

One way to identify opportunities in fixed income is to look at bond yields. That’s because yield, which is based on a bond's price and coupon payments, reflects total return potential.

Yields can change over time and across bond sectors. Spread, which refers to the difference between a bond’s yield and the yield of a risk-free issue with the same duration (e.g., U.S. Treasuries),1 indicates how much investors are being compensated for taking on additional credit (default) risk.

If spreads are above their long-term average (sometimes called "wide" or "cheap"), investors are being paid more to take on credit risk; if they're below their long-term average (called "narrow" or "tight"), investors are being paid less.

Disclosures

1 There's no credit spread on U.S. Treasuries, which are considered risk-free government securities, since payments are guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Agency MBS is represented by the Morgan Stanley 30-Year Conventional Current Coupon ZV Indicator, which represents the zero volatility spread for the hypothetical $100-priced 30-year conventional mortgage over time. Investment grade is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index. High yield is represented by the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Index. Municipal bonds are represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index descriptions are available here.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments. Falling rates may result in a fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Mortgage- and asset-backed securities are affected by interest rates, financial health of issuers/originators, creditworthiness of entities providing credit enhancements and the value of underlying assets. Income from tax-exempt municipal bonds or municipal bond funds may be subject to state and local taxes, and a portion of income may be subject to the federal and/or state alternative minimum tax for certain investors. Federal and state income tax rules will apply to any capital gains.

Additional Disclosures

© 2016-2025 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Use of products, materials and services available through Columbia Threadneedle Investments may be subject to approval by your home office.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and are managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC. ETFs are distributed by ALPS Distributors, Inc., member FINRA, an unaffiliated entity.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.

data.title: Japanese Assets Under Pressure Amid Uncertainty About Election, BoJ Normalization
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang | Michiel Tukker | Chris Turner

Polls suggest LDP and Komeito will struggle to retain majority

There are two main reasons for the recent surge in Japanese government bond yields: the domestic political uncertainty and the Bank of Japan's policy normalisation process. On the former, there are concerns that the current ruling coalition may not secure a majority in the upcoming election. This outcomecould increase uncertainty regarding the domestic political environment, which may affect the country's tariff negotiations with the US and its fiscal policy.

Second, expectations for a BoJ rate hike have resurfaced. A recent surge in inflation, particularly in food and related items, is likely to prompt the central bank to revise its quarterly inflation outlook, which will be released at the end of this month. As a result, it is possible that a BoJ rate hike could occur sooner than previously anticipated.

The ruling coalition needs a minimum of 50 seats out of 125 contested in order to secure a majority in the Upper House. However, recent polls indicate that there is a significant possibility that the Liberal Democratic Party and Komeito will fall short.

A 13-14 July poll by major newspaper Asahi Shimbun projects the ruling coalition will win approximately 43 seats in this election -- 34 for the LDP and 9 for Komeito -- down from earlier estimates of 48–56. When combined with their 75 uncontested seats, the coalition would fall short of the 125-seat majority threshold in the Upper House.

One very interesting dynamic this campaign season is that the far-right party, Sanseito, is gaining popularity among conservative voters. Sanseito promotes a “Japan First” policy similar to Donald Trump’s “America First” campaign. Although Sanseito currently holds just one seat, the recent poll suggests that it could potentially secure 8-17 seats, reflecting a surge in support among conservatives and protest voters. While not a direct threat to the LDP’s seat count, Sanseito’s rise poses a strategic challenge by fragmenting the right-leaning vote and reshaping the political narrative.

Election uncertainty has weighed on both JGBs and the yen. If the LDP loses, possible outcomes include PM Shigeru Ishiba stepping down, a snap election, or a new coalition government. Each outcome could complicate government policy and US trade negotiations.

Additionally, numerous populist policies were prominently featured during the election campaign.These include a 20,000 yen voucher, reductions to the consumption tax, increased childcare support, and cuts to social insurance premiums. Such measures are likely to place additional strain on fiscal policy; Japan is already among the most highly indebted countries in the world.

Japan government debt % of GDP

BoJ: Hotter-than-expected inflation fuels rate hike bets

Markets largely anticipate the BoJ will maintain its current stance at the 30-31 July meeting, although the quarterly inflation outlook may be revised up. The BoJ has communicated that despite the recent surge in food costs, underlying inflation has not yet solidified stabilised around the 2% mark. However, the food price spikes have broadly impacted other service costs. An upward revision in inflation may signal that another rate hike is on the way. The market-implied odds of a rate hike, as reflected in overnight index swaps (OIS), have climbed to 50–60% over the past week.

The July inflation data confirmed that underlying price pressures strengthened with core-core inflation, which excludes fresh food and energy, rising quite rapidly.

Given the tight labour market, evidenced by a roughly 2.5% unemployment rate and wage growth exceeding 5% for a second year, a shift towards expansionary fiscal policy is likely to elevate inflation expectations. This may lead to a further increase in JGB yields.

Core inflation excluding fresh food and energy has accelerated since last summer

Uncertainty over US trade policy is a key factor in the BoJ’s rate hike decisions. We expect action only after tariff issues are resolved. However, the election results will significantly slow trade talks with the US. Although US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and PM Ishiba are scheduled to meet pre-election, it is not anticipated that any concrete agreements will be made. Depending on the election results, future trade talks may face more challenges. We still expect the BoJ to deliver a 25bp hike in October.

Eurozone government bonds may offer attractive alternatives to JGBs

Fiscal uncertainty, driven by campaign promises and rising debt, may encourage Japanese investors to look elsewhere for better risk-return opportunities, with FX-hedging costs moving in their favour. Japanese institutional investors, such as pension funds and insurance firms, are known to rely on FX hedges to manage their currency exposure. The hedging costs are often derived from the relatively short-dated interest rates between two countries. Rate hikes by the Bank of Japan and cuts by the European Central Bank thereby reduce the costs to hedge euro exposures. Since the beginning of this year, the 30-year Italian government bond (BTP) has offered a higher yield on an FX-hedged basis. A 25bp hike from the BoJ and a 25bp cut by the ECB later this year would increase FX-hedged yields on euro bonds by another 50bp. If JGBs were to match such moves to balance the relative value, another 50bp rise in 30Y yields is not unthinkable.

FX-hedged eurozone bonds are increasingly appealing as an alternative to JGBs

JPY: Temporary setback

It's fair to say the yen has been underperforming recently. EUR/JPY, for instance, has rallied 6% from the start of June. A variety of factors have been at play here, including the above fiscal worries, US trade winds turning against Japan and a low-volatility environment that has favoured the carry trade. Notably, a survey we conducted during our recent FX webinar revealed that the yen had dropped dramatically, compared to the euro and the Swiss franc, as a preferred safe haven in the event of a dollar crisis.

Should the weekend election result create more domestic uncertainty for Japan and JGBs, USD/JPY could briefly push through the 149.50/150.00 area towards 151.50/152.00. However, the yen is one of the most undervalued currencies in the G10 space. We suspect that Japanese investors would use the opportunity of a stronger dollar in the third quarter to increase their FX hedges on dollar assets.

If the BoJ resumes hiking in October and the Federal Reserve cuts in December -- and the market is speculating about Fed Chair Jerome Powell's replacement at a time of seasonal dollar weakness -- then USD/JPY should be back at levels near 140 by the end of the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Shurgard Self Storage: Stable Business With Visible Growth Roadmap
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Shurgard Self Storage Ltd. (OTCPK:SSSAF) as I believe its scaled position in a fragmented market, visible growth outlook, and strong balance sheet create an attractive setup. SSSAF should be able to continue to grow EBITDA as guided, and at 18x forward EBITDA, the upside is pretty decent.

Company description

SSSAF is a self-storage operator in Europe, with 335 stores that occupy ~1.7 million sqm of rentable space in seven countries. What sets SSSAF apart is that its footprint is concentrated in densely populated urban regions like the UK, France, Germany, and the Nordics. About 80% of SSSAF's client base is residential, and revenue is primarily rental-based, with supplementary revenue from products like insurance and add-on services.

Dominant Position in a Fragmented Market

SSSAF's position as the largest player in the European self-storage market is a very powerful competitive moat, and it is because the industry is highly fragmented. In an industry where smaller, less sophisticated players are common, SSSAF scale allows it to invest aggressively to differentiate itself. The clearest example of this is its mobile app, which lets users find a unit, complete a lease, and manage their accounts without ever needing to interact with staff. I don't know about you, but being able to do this without dialing and being put on hold is a tremendous value add. According to a 2024 industry survey, only 14% of operators in Europe offered app functionality. In contrast, about half of SSSAF customers actively use theirs. This tells me the consumers clearly value having an app to manage their units.

The positive impact goes beyond just making consumers' lives easier; I actually see this as an edge because it allows SSSAF to win at the top of the funnel. I say this because most self-storage inquiries now start online (~67% per CBRE report), and as such, having this mobile app that enables consumers to easily download, navigate, and convert gives SSSAF a larger share of traffic versus smaller competitors who may not even have modern websites.

Aside from being able to invest in digital capabilities, SSSAF's "cluster" strategy is another advantage, where it places multiple stores in dense urban areas. The obvious benefit is that it gives customers more options in the same city. At the same time, this also increases route density internally-SSSAF requires fewer staff to manage more locations since they are in the same area. SSSAF also enjoys more efficient marketing spending due to this since 1 marketing campaign for 1 region can cover multiple units vs. multiple campaigns across multiple regions. Overall, this allows SSSAF to stay competitive on cost and thus price. As SSSAF scales, its ability to do both of these should continue to go up (for the latter, it has better financial power to outbid sub-scale players).

Visible Growth Pipeline

SSSAF's visible growth roadmap makes the narrative more attractive. As a refresher, management has guided a multi-year plan that targets ~25% growth in total rentable space and ~50% growth in all-store revenue by 2029 vs. 2024. This topline momentum is expected to translate into ~55% growth in underlying EBITDA and ~50% growth in adj. EPRA earnings over the same period. The key here is that this isn't just some numbers management plucked from the air. These targets are already backed by a secured pipeline of 47 projects worth EUR515 million, which are set to open between 2025 and 2027 at an 8-9% target yield-on-cost. 9% of EUR515 million is EUR46 million, which itself already represents ~20% of FY24 EBITDA (before valuation gains). Assuming SSSAF scales up and replicates something similar between 2028 and 2029, the >55% growth in underlying EBITDA doesn't seem impossible.

Resilient Business Model

Regarding SSSAF business, I also like that this is inherently a very sticky business that generates highly predictable, recurring revenue. It is important to establish that self-storage is not a discretionary purchase. The purchase intent is usually driven by major life events [give examples of such events] like moving house, death (need to store items somewhere), and marriage (combination of two households leads to a surplus of belongings). These events generally don't know the broader economic cycle.

Additionally, SSSAF itself has been able to capture strong customer loyalty. To put things into perspective, the average length of stay for customers is about 42 months in 2024, and if we dig deeper, the metric is even more impressive. A significant portion of its client base, ~60%, consists of long-term customers who stay for an average of 60 months.

For investors, this translates into a low-risk growth profile where stable, recurring cash flows can continue to support dividend payouts.

Balance Sheet

As you can imagine, SSSAF's growth is capital intensive since it has to continue to build or acquire more units. Hence, having a strong balance sheet is an important thing. As of the latest filing, SSSAF's loan-to-value ratio stood at just 23.3% and has a BBB+ investment-grade credit rating from S&P. The positive impact from this can be seen from the 3.27% average interest rate that a long 7.8-year debt maturity profile has. From a defensive point of view, this means that SSSAF doesn't need to worry about funding risk for the next few years. It ensures that SSSAF can keep executing on its pipeline and long-term strategy without relying on equity raises or expensive financing. And this becomes an offensive weapon for SSSAF, as it means SSSAF has a war chest to fund development and pursue M&A opportunities.

Valuation

The easy way to value SSSAF is to assume that it can achieve management guidance. Using EBITDA as the metric to value the stock, a ~55% increase would equate to FY29 EBITDA of EUR361 million. This is slightly lower than consensus estimates of EUR370 million, but I think it is ok to be conservative here. Generally, self-storage players that have scale trade at a similar forward EBITDA multiple. We can look at StorageVault Canada, Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), and CubeSmart (CUBE). All of them trade at ~18x forward EBITDA. SSSAF may probably get some premium for being the largest player in Europe, but given that all of them enjoy a similar moat and demand outlook, I'd say valuing SSSAF at 18x seems right.

Risk

Although SSSAF enjoys a non-discretionary demand profile, a recession could limit its ability to raise rental rates, which may limit growth. Rising interest rates also pose a risk. While the current debt profile is well-managed, future refinancing could come at a higher cost and pressure earnings. On the execution side, delays in permitting or cost inflation could hurt the returns of its new developments, especially if rental growth doesn't keep pace.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for SSSAF. This is a stable business with a visible revenue stream, a strong moat, and a solid balance sheet to fund its growth. So long as SSSAF continues to execute, its scale advantage should continue to widen, and it shouldn't face an issue achieving its EBITDA guidance.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WCLD: Bets On The Next Cloud Computing Disruptors
data.description:
I believe the WisdomTree Cloud Computing Fund ETF (NASDAQ:WCLD) is a buy opportunity for investors with a medium term investment horizon who can handle volatility. The fund is well positioned to capture the next wave of cloud computing growth by investing in early stage, disruptive businesses.

In contrast to peers like the First Trust Cloud Computing ETF (SKYY) and Global X Cloud Computing ETF (CLOU), which have leveraged larger tech leaders or diversified positions, WCLD's index, the equal-weighted BVP Nasdaq Emerging Cloud Index, focuses on high-growth firms in AI analytics, cloud-native security, and data platforms.

With worldwide spending on public cloud projected to be $723.4 Bn in 2025, fueled by AI adoption, I think WCLD's concentration on disruptors such as Snowflake (SNOW) and Zscaler (ZS) makes it well-positioned to outperform as market mood shifts from mega-cap leaders to nimble innovators.

WCLD's Advantage in a Changing Cloud Landscape

WCLD's portfolio is made of 68 companies, of which 89% is concentrated in Technology Services and 11% are divided among Financials, Industrials, and Healthcare. The top 10 holding only are 20.39% of the portfolio, with clear low concentration and deliberate focus on emerging cloud players:

This equal-weighted approach, as compared with SKYY's weighted market-cap methodology with 41.33% of the portfolio in top 10 and large cap leaders like Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL), or CLOU's focused portfolio, with 42.84% in the top 10, with REITs like Digital Realty Trust (DLR), decreases exposure to any single company and raises exposure to innovative companies.

For instance, Snowflake's data platform supports scalable AI workloads, critical given 83% of enterprises concentrate on AI in sectors like finance and health care. Zscaler cloud-native security falls into a 20% YoY growth in cybersecurity infrastructure spending. Palantir's AI-driven analytics ride the wave of consolidation, as firms join forces to build out cloud capabilities.

My thesis is predicated on a market shift away from large cap tech companies, which have driven SKYY's improved 3 years return of 22.71% compared to WCLD at 8.92% and CLOU at 12.26%. (Below)

If interest rates were to increase (if cut expectations are not met by the Fed), it will be disproportionately detrimental to high valuation large caps and present opportunities for lower, undervalued firms.

WCLD's holdings, including CrowdStrike (CRWD), will capture the booming cybersecurity demands, while its low expense ratio of 0.45% (to SKYY's 0.60% and CLOU's 0.68%) is a cost-effective point.

Why WCLD Stands Out

WCLD's focus on emerging cloud companies is not a diversification play, it's a bet on the disruptors of tomorrow in a $723.4 Bn cloud economy. The trend spans from infrastructure giants to specialist platforms.

Snowflake's ability to handle AI workloads, for example, positions it to capture demand from companies that are modernizing data infrastructure, particularly in healthcare, where cloud penetration is accelerating.

Zscaler and CrowdStrike address the growing cybersecurity threat landscape, with data breaches driving 20%+ annual growth in related costs.

Palantir's AI analytics positioning underpins M&A, where smaller firms merge to compete with larger players, offering upside potential on WCLD positions.

SKYY's broader technology exposure undermines its cloud focus, with Alphabet (GOOGL) deriving a disproportionate amount of non cloud revenue. CLOU's addition of REITs renders it more exposed to real estate cycles and less exposed to software trends.

WCLD's equal-weighting structure ensures the smaller firms, like Olo (OLO) in restaurant software or Rubrik (RBRK) in data management, more influence, thus improving exposure to niche growth niches. I believe this positioning renders WCLD a cleaner bet on cloud innovation, especially with investors seeking alternatives to overhyped tech leaders.

Balancing Opportunity and Risk

WCLD's finances provide the basis for its investment potential. Its $356.6 Mn AUM and $4.5 Mn volume provide adequate liquidity, but still lagging SKYY's $3.4 Bn AUM and $21.9 Mn volume. CLOU stands at $320.1 Mn AUM and $2.9 Mn volume, being less liquid but close to WCLD. WCLD's minimal expense ratio is cost-saving for long-term investors, valuable in an uncertain economic landscape.

Risks are considerable, though. WCLD's focus on growth stocks involves risk, particularly in AI and cybersecurity areas, where temporary disappointments are possible.

Its $4.5 Mn average daily trading volume is subject to price volatility from big trades, as compared with SKYY's deeper liquidity.

Regulatory pressure, especially on data privacy for stocks like Datadog (DDOG), could raise compliance costs, impacting returns. Macroeconomic headwinds such as falling IT spending in recession provide further difficulties, though WCLD's positioning in digital transformation mitigates some of its exposure.

I believe WCLD's 20.39% top 10 holdings are a good level of diversification, reducing concentration risk compared to SKYY and CLOU's 40 to 45% top 10 weights. This positioning is protected from single stock implosions while being able to benefit from the success of numerous innovators.

Regulatory risk, while present, is less burdensome for cloud-native businesses than data-driven tech behemoths, giving WCLD an advantage in navigating changing compliance landscapes.

Looking Ahead: WCLD's Medium Term Potential

Over the next months, I expect WCLD to benefit from a trend towards undervalued cloud leaders, as large caps stocks are pressured by rising interest rates and economic volatility.

Expansion within the cloud industry, a projected $723.4 Bn in 2025, is driven by AI, cyber security, and data analytics, industries where WCLD's positions are highlighted.

For instance, Veeva Systems (VEEV) facilitates cloud based solutions for life sciences, an industry expected to utilize the cloud increasingly. Cloudflare (NET) accelerates edge computing, a growing niche with big businesses desiring low-latency solutions.

WCLD's recent underperformance (8.92% over the last 3 years) is a product of market affinity for large cap tech companies with activities away from cloud computing, but its focus on smaller companies positions it to bounce back as investors seek growth at reasonable valuations.

The fund's equal-weighted approach ensures that blowouts from the likes of Toast (TOST) in payment platforms or Rubrik in data security can power outsized returns. I anticipate WCLD's thesis to materialize in the medium term as consolidation and innovation pick up steam, fueled by M&A dynamics and increasing enterprise cloud budgets.

Conclusion

WCLD is the possibility to invest in next-generation cloud computing disruptors, not its established titans.

Its low-cost ratio, diversified portfolio, and coordination with high-growth themes such as AI and cybersecurity make it an attractive purchase for risk-adjusted investors.

While volatility and regulatory risks call for caution, WCLD's focus on early-stage businesses leaves it well positioned to beat peers like SKYY and CLOU in a market where size will be trumped by innovation. Investors looking to catch the next wave in the cloud industry should consider WCLD as a champion.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 07/18/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

data.title: Roper Technologies' Repeatable Growth Model Deserves A Premium - Especially Now
data.description:
The Buy thesis for Roper Technologies (NASDAQ:ROP) is not around hypergrowth or hype narratives. I am not expecting Roper to double in 5 years. In fact Roper has only grown at a CAGR of less than 7% over the past 5 years - at a time where the average tech world has been growing at a CAGR of 20% (proxied by VGT).

Roper does not even bring a lucrative dividend payout to the table. Its current yield of 0.6% is far from exciting, although it remains extremely stable and safe, with a payout ratio of ~16%.

In fact the conservative payout ratio is representative of the Buy thesis for Roper. A conservative and defensive growth stock that should be part of your portfolio as a stabilizer, particularly as valuation pressures mount on broader tech innovation. I believe a higher for longer rates scene, coupled with potential economic growth de-acceleration in the near to medium term will see money rotating out of some moonshots. Mega caps may hold up, if not grow, and investors will gravitate towards the safety of defensive tech beyond hype narratives. That is where Roper comes into the portfolio as a stabilizer to manage risks.

The Repeatable Promise

Roper's business model revolves around two key levers. The first lever is disciplined acquisition of software offerings that are mostly "essential" for businesses. For example, laboratory information management systems, campus operations management solutions, and financial planning and analytics. These solutions form the core of the client businesses, and that makes Roper's offerings repeatable, niche, specialized, and resistant to discretionary IT spend cuts.

Roper is continually acquiring such offerings in what is labeled as its inorganic growth avenue. The second lever is the revenue growth through new installations, improved offerings, or pricing improvements of the onboarded products - organic growth. This philosophy of acquiring "ready to deploy" solutions cuts development or adoption risks. Roper's risks are therefore far more executional in nature, restricted to maintaining and growing existing installations. Of course, the approach is not one that could catch fire tomorrow like cutting-edge analytics or AI/ML solutions. But it is reliable and resilient. Most importantly it is repeatable as has been the case over the years.

Over decades, Ropers topline growth has been predictably stable, with only minor blips at times when the rest of the tech world was bleeding. It has also pivoted steadily from industrial solutions to software during the period - but the philosophy remains. With maturity, the operating margins have also matured, and now Roper is a growing topline at ~28% healthy operating margins story.

Core Business Model Strengths

As Roper has now moved into a SaaS like model (a collection of vertical Software solutions), its recurring and reoccurring revenues are strong - supporting revenue visibility and healthy margins. In Q1 2025, recurring and reoccurring revenues were ~86.5% of total vertical SaaS revenues, compared to 85.5% a year ago.

For a model like Roper's, it is not surprising that free cash flow conversion is remarkably good and often even exceeds operating income. Although some of the FCF conversion is potentially attributable to deferred revenue inflow from long-term contracts, the core conversion levers - from a low capex (software, that too onboarding matured products cuts out development costs and makes the model asset-light) and stable working capital (boosted by significant recurring revenues) - are structural.

The healthy cash flow also makes the debt leverage manageable. For a acquisition based business model, a Net Debt to EBITDA ratio of around 2.5x is very reasonable. Roper is very disciplined around its leverage and keeps deleveraging itself periodically as seen from its stable debt levels. The acquisitions are funded through cash and debt primarily.

A less than 7% dilution in shares outstanding in the last 10 years, with no strong buyback policy, is modest dilution at best. Looking at the stock based compensation, I assume a total of ~$1.2b worth of shares diluted through that route. A very rough estimate (encouraged by the almost straight line price behavior of the stock) of an average price of ~$360 for the period implies around 3.33m shares diluted through compensation. That works out to be around half of the share count increase in the period.

Another ~$1.2b is potentially used for equity based acquisitions - that should not be more than 5-10% of the acquisition capital requirements in the past 10 years. So, that brings us back to acquisitions (a key lever in the business model) funded primarily through debt (access to a $3.5b revolver line) and cash. Debt leverage is maintained through the healthy cash generated and some cash may also be used for the acquisitions.

The flywheel of acquisition fueled growth, fueling further acquisitions, looks self sustainable to a large extent, and debt exposures are contained relatively conservatively. The growth flywheel feeds of both inorganic revenues (businesses acquired contribute to revenues from day one) and a healthy organic revenue base.

In the past 5 years, topline has grown at a CAGR of ~9.5% through this flywheel effect. Even in Q1 2025, a 12% YoY topline growth was reported. Organic growth was around 5% and inorganic growth was 8% - a good balance of the double engine growth story.

Risks to the Thesis

The thesis hinges on Roper being able to repeat what it has been doing so far. Poor acquisitions at the wrong price is a constant risk that Roper has mitigated well so far with disciplined execution. Investors should keep an eye on sustained organic growth below 5% because that weakens the flywheel. In a risk-on market, Roper's valuations could actually be further compressed - not likely in today's environment, but a risk. Past share price performance does not indicate high risk here though.

Why Roper and Why Now

Roper has historically traded at around ~25-30x in terms of the price to free cash flow ratio. With a current ratio of ~26x, it is below its own modest highs of ~30x (as per YCharts). Even in Seeking Alpha, the price to TTM cash flow ratio is ~24.5x, below its 5 year average of ~33x. For a business with 31% FCF margin (as per the Q1 2025 report) and a predictable and stable cash flow, the odds of rerating purely on shifting market sentiment towards defensive tech is high.

And that is more the case now with stretched valuations and macro uncertainties pressuring growth names in tech. If Roper is trading at below ~30x price to cash flow and an uncertain market scenario is looming, the case for a Buy is very Strong in my opinion. If not the defensive value eventually coming to the fore in the event of corrections, a simple rerating upside of 20% with stable cash flows offers that much upside in share prices as an optionality.

And in the event of a drawdown, Ropers resilience places it a very strong position. Even in the recent tariff sell-off, when broader tech was down by 25% on average, Roper almost held its ground and eventually cracked by only 7%. A strong tech portfolio stabilizer with decent upside optionality - a Strong Buy in the current context.

Roper is well positioned for the year with guidance suggesting 12% topline growth (upgraded from 10% in Q1 2025). EPS guidance is roughly maintained from earlier estimates. In the upcoming earnings, I expect the impacts of the recent acquisition (CentralReach - an autism therapy enabler) to start to how up.

CentralReach is expected to contribute $175m in revenues by Q2 2026 (~3% of Roper's total revenues). EBITDA expectations are ~$75m. Initial contributions and indications from the management around CentralReach's impact will be closely watched, and any positives there could boost share prices even in the short run. I am also expecting margin stability or modest expansion especially in Application Software. Organic growth should also stay near the 6% mark. These could drive sentiment around prices more than overall beats and misses on EPS, because Roper is about the core flywheel that works on growth, margins and quality acquisitions. As long as those core levers look supportive, Roper should do well. Risks are limited.

data.title: Western Alliance: Solid Q2 But Reserve Concerns Remain
data.description:
Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 16%. It has been a bumpy ride with shares still below their post-election euphoric high but also well above the lows amidst peak tariff fears. I last covered shares in April, rating the stock a "hold." Since then, it has rallied 28%, beating the market's 21% gain, suggesting a buy rating would have been merited. Given how much shares have rallied and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit WAL.

In the company's second quarter, Western Alliance earned $2.07, beating estimates by $0.04. Earnings were up strongly from $1.75 last year as WAL has returned to growth after facing severe pressure in the 2023 regional banking crisis. Aside from its traditional banking business, Western Alliance has a large mortgage warehousing and servicing business, which is a critical source of low-cost deposits. Thanks to this business, WAL generated $148 million in noninterest-income, up $33 million from last year, due to higher loan servicing fees and a gain on sale of investment securities.

Balance sheet growth is strong

In an industry with muted balance sheet growth, WAL stands out as a clear exception. In Q2, deposits grew $1.8 billion to $71 billion, up 7.3% from last year. As you can see below, Western Alliance's deposit base has undergone significant volatility last year. Given its reliance on the tech sector and its high share of noninterest-bearing (NIB) balances, its deposit base came under severe pressure during early 2023. Its strong capital and liquidity position allowed it to weather the storm, and as markets grew comfortable with its underlying health, you can see that its business recovered markedly to the point where deposits are now substantially higher than they were pre-crisis.

As you can see below, it has seen the share of NIB balances decline over the past year, but only modestly. Sequentially, $988 million increase in noninterest-bearing deposits, which is in keeping with typical patterns given the seasonality of mortgage activity. I have been encouraged to see WAL steadily reduce its share of CDs. During 2023, WAL had to turn to higher-cost funding to stabilize its deposit base, and these long-term, higher-cost deposits have weighed on net interest margins. As these roll off, WAL's margin performance should improve.

Indeed, that is exactly what is occurring. Interest-bearing deposit cost of 3.19% were down 7 bps sequentially, given the maturity of high-cost CDs. Outstanding CDs cost 4.38%, down from a peak over 5%. As a result of this, deposit costs are down 54 bps from last year. Reflecting a strong pull-through of the Fed's 100bps of rate costs. We have likely seen the vast majority of deposit pricing improvement, but even at a flat Fed funds rate, I would expect WAL's deposit costs to continue declining, likely about 10bps.

Loan growth is strong but reserves are low

At the same time, given growing deposits, Western Alliance's loan balances grew by $1.2 billion to $56 billion, up 6.7% from last year. Two-thirds of loan growth was due to increased business lending, which is the primary focus of its lending operation. WAL carries $25 billion of C&I loans. It has just $10.3 billion in investor commercial real estate loans. At less than 20% of loans, this is an unusually small exposure in the regional banking sector, which I view positively given the structural challenges facing the sector. This balance is up just about 6% from last year, in line with total loan growth.

Credit quality remains solid, given the favorable composition of its loan book, though I do continue to believe reserves are relatively low. Indeed, during the quarter, WAL took $40 million in provisions for credit losses, up $9 million from Q1. Most banks should report sequential drops in provisions, given the better economic outlook as tariff fears have subsided, but WAL's lower reserve position may leave it continuing to add reserves. During the quarter, it took 22 bps of charge-offs, up from 20ps last quarter and 18 bps a year ago. The company has 76bps of nonperforming loans, which is barely covered by its 78bps of allowances, up 1bp from last quarter.

The company has $395 million in allowances, up $6 million sequentially and $43 million from a year ago. While much of its loan portfolio is protected by SBA guarantees and other forms of credit protection, which greatly reduces its loss-given default relative to other banks, this is very low coverage. I like to see at least 150% coverage. Absent a recession, which would cause NPLs to spike, I would not expect a wholesale change in WAL's reserve approach. Rather, I expect provisions to continue to run at the ~$40 million level, even as some peers may release reserves.

Its securities portfolio is relatively well positioned

Aside from its loan book, WAL also has $18.6 billion in investment securities, up $2.7 billion sequentially from $1.3 billion from last year. Unlike most banks, which have securities portfolios with large unrealized losses from buying bonds when rates were lower, WAL keeps a much shorter duration, which has left its securities portfolio in a far better position. Indeed whereas many peers have yields in the 3-3.5% range, WAL has a 4.81% investment securities yield. This is actually down 6 bps from last year due to lower yields on its floating rate securities.

The magnitude of the growth in its securities portfolio is actually surprising to me. Because its asset growth outstripped its deposit growth, WAL now has $6.1 billion in borrowings, up $1.2 billion sequentially. Given the relatively high cost of wholesale borrowing, I would like to see WAL work this down below $5 billion again by allowing securities to roll off its balance sheet or grow deposits more quickly than loans. Indeed, it may be starting to focus on this as it has a 78.7% loan to deposit ratio, down 40bps from last year.

Given improved deposit pricing, WAL had a 3.53% net interest margin, up 6bps sequentially. However, this was down 10 bps from last year given the decline in the securities portfolio yield. Still even with a lower margin, net interest income grew by 6% to $698 million thanks to the growth in the size of its balance sheet. This growth also outpaced the 5.7% growth in noninterest expense as it expanded headcount to support growth. Assuming no more than two fed rate cuts this year, I would expect NIM to move towards 3.6% in H2.

Capital is strong, and shares are fairly valued

Importantly, Western Alliance remains well capitalized with a common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 11.2%. This is especially strong given the positioning of its securities portfolio. Given this capital positioning, I view its dividend as secure, offering shares a 1.9% yield. Alongside results, the company announced a new CFO with the existing CFO moving to a new role, focused on deposits. This appears to be an orderly transition, and accordingly, I do not expect it to result in a change in financial policy. I continue to expect WAL to use capital to support balance sheet growth with a relatively low focus on buybacks.

Back in April, I was targeting $8.00-$8.50 in EPS. WAL continues to perform broadly in-line with my expectations, though fears of a recession have subsided, making its reserve position less of a risk than three months ago. Accordingly, I expect earnings to be $8.20-$8.60 this year. Given its reserves and reliance on elevated NIB balances, I view fair value as no more than 10x earnings. This would also leave shares about 1.5x its $56 book value, which I view as broadly fair given a mid-teens return on tangible equity. As such, I view shares around fair value.

Accordingly, I rate WAL a hold. Its share price recovery has largely occurred, and I would not expect these results to be a catalyst driving more upside. At the same time, at 10x, shares are not overvalued, meaning there is no rush to sell. I see shares as around fair value and likely to be a market performer, meriting a "hold."

data.title: CHY: Not The Ideal Time For This Convertible Fund
data.description:
Overview

Calamos Convertible & High Income Fund (NASDAQ:CHY) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of convertible securities and high-yield corporate bonds. Income funds like CHY can be an effective way for investors to hedge their portfolios against the volatility experienced from traditional equities in the market. The consistent income received from these sort of funds can improve investor confidence and make it easier to remain invested through market uncertainty. CHY has an inception dating back to 2003 and has a management fee of 0.8%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CHY's price has decreased by about 5.1%. The fund has been on a decline since the start of the year, and I believe there may be more downside price action ahead as the debt market becomes less resilient due to higher interest rates. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 5.6% over the same period. CHY offers investors a generous starting dividend yield of 11%, and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. This makes CHY an enticing choice for investors that are seeking a source of reliable dividend income.

Despite the fund's strengths and diversity of investments, I am concerned about shifts in the macroeconomic environment that can negatively impact earnings. If interest rates decline, the fund's net investment income levels can decline. However, the health of the portfolio can decline in a scenario where interest rates are not cut by the end of the year. Both scenarios can lead to the possibility of a dividend cut. Additionally, CHY currently trades at the higher end of its price to NAV range. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that CHY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, CHY has total managed assets of $1.3B that is spread across a diverse range of income securities. The fund utilizes a range of different securities that put an emphasis on income generation rather than growth. CHY is most heavily concentrated on convertible securities. As the name implies, these securities operate as a hybrid instrument that is structure like a bond at first, but can be converted to common shares if management believes the security has the potential for valuation growth. The breakdown by asset allocation is as follows:

  • Convertibles: 66.9%
  • Corporate Bonds: 24.6%
  • Bank Loans: 4.9%
  • Cash & Receivables/Payables: 2.8%
  • Common Stock: 0.6%
  • Preferred Stock: 0.1%
  • Options: 0.1%
  • Asset Backed: 0.1%

CHY also maintains an efficient level of diversification across sectors, with Information Technology consisting of 27.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Consumer Discretionary and Industrial investments, accounting for 12.5% and 10.3% of assets respectively. The combination of the top three sectors account for 50% of total assets, so we should expect these to have the greatest influence on CHY's daily price change and total return potential.

Furthermore, CHY maintains some exposure to very notable large-cap companies. For instance, Boeing Company (BA) is the largest position at 2.2% of assets. This is followed by other notable names like Snowflake (SNOW), Uber Technologies (UBER), and Strategy (MSTR) just to name a few. However, these positions are structured as convertible securities and not as common shares. Therefore, CHY has not directly participated in the growth of these underlying holdings.

Despite the convertible nature of its positions, underperformance from a price perspective has been a consistent issue for the fund. Just to demonstrate, I pulled in a performance chart for CHY over the last ten year period. We can see that CHY's price has declined by about 17.9% during this time span. However, distributions paid out over the last ten years boost the total return above 116%. Based on its past performance, investors should have very limited expectations for capital appreciation to occur with a fund like CHY.

Valuation & Risk

Since CHY operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, CHY currently trades at a very small premium to NAV of 0.7%. However, this premium is almost immaterial when we consider that CHY has traded at an average premium to NAV of 7.28% over the last three-year period. However, I believe that the higher premium valuation can be attributed to the higher interest rate environment.

Higher interest rates meant that funds that utilized different debt securities were able to collect elevated interest incomes from their portfolio. This made income funds like CHY more attractive as investors flocked to these assets as a way to capitalize on higher rates. However, the latest semi-annual report for 2025 indicates that CHY's internal performance didn't actually improve. For instance net investment income still amounted to a loss of $0.05 per share, while there were also net realized losses of $0.11 per share.

Looking at prior years reveals that the fund has more frequently experienced losses in net investment income since the pandemic of 2020. Prior to the pandemic, CHY generated a pretty consistent level of net investment income between a range of $0.49 to $0.62 per share. The fluctuating interest rate environment could have made it difficult for the fund's management to continue operating at a regular capacity and created an environment where it's more difficult to locate profitable opportunities. As a result, the NAV has suffered in recent years.

  • 2020: $12.54 per share
  • 2021: $15.49 per share
  • 2022: $10.26 per share
  • 2023: $8.99 per share
  • 2024: $10.04 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $9.32 per share

The underwhelming performance can also be attributed to the fact that CHY utilizes a heavy rate of leverage in an effort to amplify returns. The use of leverage currently amounts to 34.93% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $458M. Leverage can amplify gains when allocated towards attractive opportunities, but the latest reporting seems to indicate that this hasn't been the case. Investors must also recognize the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

For reference, interest rates still remain near their decades high, which can make it more difficult for leverage to be used effectively. Since the cost of debt is higher, this shrinks the spread between the cost of leverage and the yield that is generated from underlying securities. The Fed has continued to keep interest rates unchanged as they try to monitor the impact of tariffs, inflationary data, unemployment levels, and consumer spending. If interest rates remain elevated, this can keep CHY's performance underwhelming and keep the share price suppressed.

Higher interest rates can also increase the possibility for defaults to occur. As part of its portfolio strategy, CHY maintains exposure to investments with poor credit ratings. For reference, borrowers with a credit rating below BBB are considered to be below investment grade. These below investment grade borrowers usually have less consistent cash flows, may be more reliant on debt, and might be less resilient to a change in consumer spending habits. The breakdown by rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 14.2%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - CCC and Below): 26.7%
  • Not Rated: 59.2%

If interest rates remain elevated, the rate of leveraged loan defaults can increase over the next twelve months. For instance, S&P Global estimates that defaults can see an uptick to 1.75% by March of 2026 in their base scenario. This assumes slower economic growth and rising prices that challenges borrowers. However, S&P Global has a pessimistic scenario where they see defaults increased to 3.5% by March 2026 if there are additional trade disruptions related to tariffs.

While I do think that performance can improve, it ultimately comes down to when interest rates are cut. I think that the fund will perform a lot better in a lower interest rate environment, which allows for better operating margins and a lower cost of debt. A lower rate environment can also make it easier for borrowers to appreciate in value over time, since a lower portion of earnings need to be allocated towards debt repayment.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.10 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.20 per share. Although the fund has a history of keeping payouts consistent over the last decade, I believe that there's a chance that CHY will reduce its dividend at some point in the near term if performance doesn't improve. Referencing the same semi-annual report, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $2.86 per share
  • 2021: $4.05 per share
  • 2022: ($4.10) per share
  • 2023: ($0.13) per share
  • 2024: $2.19 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($0.16) per share

While the NAV hasn't seen as drastic of a decline yet, management may not be looking to reduce the payouts at this point. However, I would argue that it would be beneficial to long-term investors if the dividend was cut proactively, rather than at a time when it was required and a reactive move due to poor performance. A cut would help better preserve the NAV during this time of weak performance and allow the fund to experience a greater amount of capital appreciation when things do improve. The cut wouldn't have to be permanent, but it can be a way to temporarily offset the weaker performance.

Additionally, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from CHY may not be tax-efficient. The latest distribution breakdown indications that the income is being fueled by the combination of short-term capital gains and return of capital. Short-term capital gains are classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. The usage of return of capital is also an indication that CHY isn't generating enough income to support the payouts at the moment.

Takeaway

In conclusion, CHY has performed poorly in recent years due to the fluctuations in the interest rate environment. The fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify returns, but the latest semi-annual report indicates that CHY has been struggling to maintain a consistent earnings streak since 2020. I do think that the fund can offer a compelling value for investors, but only if interest rates are eventually cut. However, there's a risk that the Fed doesn't cut rates soon, and this can cause CHY's valuation to continue its downtrend. Higher rates can lead to higher defaults, and this would cause CHY's price to decline into the discount to NAV territory.
